nativeRange.habitat: Streams, tributaries, floodplain lakes, and backwaters of the Río de la Plata basin; generally prefers smaller watercourses over major river channels

decorPreferences: robust or artificial plants (live plants will be eaten)

diet.notes: Accepts dried flake and granule foods readily; tends to benefit from regular supplements of frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, Artemia, and mosquito larvae. Will also consume plant material — live aquarium plants are at risk of being eaten, so robust or artificial alternatives are generally advised.

avoidWith: long-finned fish (e.g. Angelfish, fancy guppies, bettas, longfin danios)

summary: A hardy, active schooling tetra from the Río de la Plata basin, generally considered beginner-friendly due to its wide environmental tolerance — but best suited to species-appropriate community tanks, as it tends to nip fins and consume live plants.
careNotes:
The Buenos Aires Tetra's scientific name has seen some revision: post-2020 phylogenetic work reclassified the species to Psalidodon anisitsi, though Hyphessobrycon anisitsi remains the name most commonly encountered in the aquarium trade. The common name is also somewhat misleading — the species does not appear to be naturally endemic to Buenos Aires Province, and some records from that province are now attributed to the similar-looking H. togoi. Its natural distribution covers the broader Paraná and Uruguay river systems across Argentina, southeastern Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay.

The Buenos Aires Tetra is one of the more cold-tolerant tetras and has been reported to survive in unheated aquariums in reasonably insulated homes, though it generally shows less vivid coloration at lower temperatures. Sustained maintenance toward the upper end of the temperature range (above 26°C) is generally not advised for long-term health.

Fin-nipping is a well-documented tendency in this species, and the behaviour tends to be most pronounced toward slow-moving or long-finned tankmates such as Angelfish, fancy guppies, Bettas, Sailfin Mollies, and Longfin Danios. Keeping the species in larger groups (8–10 or more) and ensuring it is well-fed have been reported to reduce nipping frequency. The Buenos Aires Tetra is also well-documented as a plant consumer; live plants tend to be uprooted and eaten, and planted-tank keepers generally find it incompatible with soft-leaved species. Very hardy plants such as Java Fern and Anubias, or artificial plants, are typically recommended instead.

Selectively bred variants include an albino morph (pink-eyed, white-bodied) and a longfin morph. These captive-bred forms tend to be somewhat more delicate than wild-type individuals, and longfin males are themselves susceptible to fin damage from conspecifics or tankmates.

The species has reportedly established feral or invasive populations in some warmer-water regions outside its native range, apparently facilitated by aquarium releases, though documented invasive records are sparse.

Adult size varies across sources: hobby literature typically cites 5–8 cm in aquarium conditions, with exceptional specimens approaching 10 cm, while wild-caught specimens have been recorded at a maximum total length of 13.2 cm. Well-grown aquarium adults are generally in the 7–10 cm range.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer with no parental care. Spawning tends to occur in the morning and is triggered by slightly elevated temperature, dim lighting, and the presence of fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. A well-conditioned female may produce several hundred to around 2,000 eggs per spawning event. Eggs typically hatch within 20–36 hours and fry become free-swimming after 3–4 days. Parents will consume eggs and fry if not removed immediately after spawning. Breeding is generally considered easy relative to many characins. The albino and longfin captive-bred strains can require more effort to bring into spawning condition.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op, which has no dedicated species care guide for this species (only forum discussions). SeriouslyFish is the first listed secondary for fish-freshwater and is authoritative for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority; confirms Psalidodon anisitsi reclassification, maximum TL 13.2 cm, native range in Paraná and Uruguay river basins. Used for taxonomy, size, and range cross-verification.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Buenos Aires tetra
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range, adult size (~7.5 cm), lifespan (5–6 yr), and invasive/conservation status. Confirmed genus reclassification.

additional.name: Aquadiction
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (17–28°C, pH 5.8–8.5, gH 2–35), adult size (7 cm), tank size (30 gal / ~114 L), lifespan (up to 10 yr), and fin-nipping/plant-eating behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg scatterer that tends to spawn readily in captivity when provided with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, slightly elevated temperature, and dim lighting. Parents will consume eggs and fry if not removed immediately after spawning.
